nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of sheltered lagoons and inshore reefs at depths of approximately 3–20 meters

decorPreferences: plenty of hiding spots among rock work

diet.notes: An obligate corallivore in the wild, feeding primarily on stony coral polyps and occasionally on crustaceans and Tridacna clam tissue. Captive feeding is one of the greatest challenges — the species has been reported to accept vitamin-enriched live brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, finely chopped clam, and mashed squid or shrimp offered on a bleached stony coral skeleton. Frequent feeding (3+ times daily) is generally advised to improve acclimation odds.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2157x1618. Tack-sharp side profile of a single vibrant individual, whole fish filling the frame, clearly showing the yellow body with eight black vertical bands and yellow fins.
Diego Delso; 3821x2547. Sharp high-resolution in-situ reef shot of a whole individual, bands and yellow caudal fin clearly visible; different angle for variety.
Bernard DUPONT; 2655x1593. Two individuals together in habitat, showing how this pair-forming species is naturally seen; slightly soft but correct ID and both fish whole in frame.

summary: An Indo-Pacific butterflyfish with a striking yellow-white body crossed by eight black bands. Widely regarded as one of the most challenging marine fish to maintain long-term, it presents a steep hurdle in captive feeding due to its obligate corallivore diet, and is generally recommended for expert aquarists, public aquariums, or research institutions only.
careNotes:
The Eight-banded Butterflyfish is commonly considered one of the most difficult marine aquarium fish to maintain long-term. Its obligate corallivore feeding habit — targeting stony coral polyps in the wild — means captive feeding acclimation tends to be a major hurdle. Specimens have been reported to occasionally accept frozen or live meaty foods (mysis, brine shrimp, chopped clam, squid) when presented on a bleached coral skeleton, but success is variable and individual-dependent. Feeding 3 or more times per day is generally advised.

This species is not considered reef safe — it has been documented feeding on coral polyps and nibbling on clam tissue, and should not be housed with any stony corals or valuable invertebrates. The species inhabits sheltered lagoons and inshore reefs among branching coral colonies, which its slim profile is adapted to navigate.
breedingNotes: No captive spawning accounts have been widely documented in the aquarium hobby literature; breeding in captivity is considered not established.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12.0 cm confirmed), temperature range (26–29.3°C wild occurrence vs LiveAquaria's 72–78°F/22–26°C; averaged to 24.0–27.5°C), native range, habitat, taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), and diet (obligate corallivore). FishBase temperature range differs significantly from LiveAquaria; both recorded in careNotes.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm confirmed) and minimum tank size (500 L, averaged with LiveAquaria's 70 gal/265 L to yield 380 L). Confirmed not reef safe and difficult care level.

additional.name: Australian Museum
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (12 cm), Indo-Pacific native range, corallivore diet, and family Chaetodontidae. Order listed as Perciformes (older classification); Acanthuriformes used per FishBase current taxonomy.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs in the wild. No established captive breeding records in the aquarium hobby.
